content.text: Winnie the Pooh By A A Milne Once upon a time, a very long time ago now, about last Friday, Christopher Robin came to the Forest to visit his friend Winnie-the-Pooh.
Pooh lived in a grand house in the middle of a wood, under the name of Sanders—which meant that above his door was written in gold letters: "Mr. Sanders." But nobody knew quite why.
One fine morning, Pooh woke up with a rumble in his tummy. He sat up, scratched his head, and thought very hard. "What do I like best in the world?" he asked himself.
And after a little while, he answered, "Honey!" So off he went to the cupboard to fetch a small pot of honey. But alas! The pot was empty. "Oh, bother," said Pooh.
"I must go and visit someone who has honey." He put on his boots, his coat, and his thinking cap (which was really just his usual cap, but it helped him think), and set off through the Forest.
The sun was shining, the birds were singing, and Pooh hummed a little hum as he walked. Soon, he came to a tall, tall tree with a buzzing sound coming from the top. "Bees!"
said Pooh excitedly. "And where there are bees, there is honey!" Now, Pooh was not a very clever bear—some might even say he was a Bear of Very Little Brain—but he was very good at
climbing. So he began to climb the tree, humming all the way. But the bees were not pleased. They buzzed angrily around him, and Pooh realized he had made a mistake.
"Perhaps," he thought, "I should have brought a balloon." Just then, Christopher Robin came walking by. "Hallo, Pooh!" he said. "What are you doing up there?"
"I’m getting honey," said Pooh. "But the bees don’t seem to like it." Christopher Robin laughed. "Come down, silly old bear. Let’s go and ask Rabbit if he has any honey."
So Pooh climbed down (which was much harder than climbing up), and together they walked to Rabbit’s house. Rabbit lived in a cozy burrow with a round green door.
When he saw Pooh and Christopher Robin, he invited them inside for tea. "Would you like some honey?" asked Rabbit. "Oh, yes, please!" said Pooh, licking his lips.
Rabbit brought out a big pot of honey, and Pooh ate and ate and ate. He ate so much that when he tried to stand up, he couldn’t fit through the door! "Oh, dear," said Pooh.
"I think I’m stuck." "Nonsense!" said Rabbit. "Just wiggle a bit." But Pooh couldn’t wiggle. He was stuck fast. "This is a fine pickle," said Christopher Robin.
"We’ll have to wait until you get thin again." So Pooh stayed stuck in Rabbit’s doorway for a whole week.
Every day, Rabbit would bring him food (mostly honey, because that’s what Pooh liked best), and every day, Pooh would sigh and say, "I do wish I could get out."
Finally, after seven days, Pooh gave a great big wiggle—and pop!—out he came! "Hooray!" cheered Christopher Robin. "I’m free!" cried Pooh. "Now, where’s my honey?"
One snowy morning, Pooh woke up feeling very adventurous. He put on his boots and went to visit his best friend, Piglet. "Hallo, Piglet!" said Pooh. "Let’s go on an expedition!"
"What sort of expedition?" asked Piglet nervously. "A hunting expedition!" said Pooh. "We’ll track a Woozle!" "A what?" said Piglet. "A Woozle," said Pooh. "It’s a sort of animal.
I’ve never seen one, but I’m sure they’re around here somewhere." So off they went, following footprints in the snow.
The footprints went round and round a tree, and Pooh and Piglet followed them, getting more and more excited. "Look!" whispered Pooh. "More footprints!
That means there are two Woozles!" "Oh, dear," said Piglet. "What if they’re dangerous?" Just then, Christopher Robin came along. "What are you doing?" he asked. "Hunting Woozles!"
said Pooh. Christopher Robin laughed. "Those are your footprints, Pooh! You’ve been walking in a circle!" Pooh scratched his head. "Oh. Well, never mind.
Let’s go home and have some honey." One day, Pooh was walking through the Forest when he saw his friend Eeyore standing by himself, looking very sad. "Hallo, Eeyore," said Pooh.
"What’s the matter?" "Nothing," said Eeyore gloomily. "Nobody cares about me. My tail is gone, and nobody even noticed." "Your tail is gone?" said Pooh. "Oh, dear! Let’s find it!"
So Pooh and Eeyore set off to search for the missing tail. They looked under bushes, behind trees, and even in Rabbit’s burrow (though Rabbit said he hadn’t seen it).
Finally, Pooh had an idea. "Let’s ask Owl," he said. "He knows everything." Owl lived in a grand treehouse with a knocker on the door. When Pooh knocked, Owl came out.
"Good morning, Owl," said Pooh. "Have you seen Eeyore’s tail?" Owl thought for a moment. "Ah, yes! I have it right here. I use it as a bell-pull."
And sure enough, there was Eeyore’s tail, hanging by the door! "Oh, thank you, Owl!" said Eeyore, though he still looked a little sad. "You’re welcome," said Owl.
"Now, would you like some tea?" One sunny afternoon, Christopher Robin decided to give a party for all his friends.
He invited Pooh, Piglet, Rabbit, Owl, Eeyore, and even Kanga and Roo. "It’s a expotition party!" said Christopher Robin. "That means we’re going on an adventure!"
So they all set off to a beautiful spot in the Forest where Christopher Robin had set up a picnic. There were sandwiches, honey cakes, and even a special pot of honey just for Pooh.
"This is the best party ever!" said Pooh, licking his lips. "I’m glad you like it," said Christopher Robin. "But the best part is yet to come!" Just then, he pulled out a big bunch of balloons.
"Who wants to go up in the air?" "Me! Me!" cried Piglet. So Christopher Robin tied a balloon to Piglet, and up he went, floating gently above the trees. "Wheee!" squeaked Piglet.
Pooh watched enviously. "I want to go too!" So Christopher Robin tied a balloon to Pooh, and up he went—higher and higher—until he was floating among the clouds!
"This is wonderful!" said Pooh. "But I do wish I had some honey." And so the party went on, with laughter and games and happiness, until the sun began to set.
"Time to go home," said Christopher Robin. And so they all said goodbye, promising to have another adventure very soon.
And that, my dear friends, is the story of Winnie-the-Pooh—the Bear of Very Little Brain, but with the biggest heart in the Hundred Acre Wood.
